chunks.text: particularly considering evidence from a meta-analysis of 21 sham- controlled rTMS trials for auditory hallucinations in schizophrenia, which reported a small but statistically significant placebo effect (Hed - ges ’ g = 0.29). Interestingly, the magnitude of the placebo response varied considerably depending on study design and the type of sham stimulation used ( Dollfus et al., 2016 ). Therefore, this single-case report cannot establish causality or support generalization. The rapid improvement may reflect, in part, non-specific factors (e.g., expect - ancy/placebo) and concurrent clinical care. However, in our case, the changes observed in the sLORETA analysis following rTMS treatment support the conclusion that the clinical improvement reflects a genuine neuromodulatory effect of rTMS. The targeted area — the left auditory cortex — and functionally connected parietal regions, particularly Brod - mann Area 40 in the parietal lobe, showed significant post-stimulation reductions in beta and gamma band activity.

chunks.file_name: Provaznikova - 2026 - Remission of auditory verbal hallucinations following neuronavigated, personalized rTMS guided by EEG, MRI and auditory evoked potentials - a case report.pdf
chunks.title: Provaznikova 2026 Remission of auditory verbal hallucinations following neuronavigated, personalized rTMS guided by EEG, MRI and auditory evoked potentials a case report
chunks.text: and functionally connected parietal regions, particularly Brod - mann Area 40 in the parietal lobe, showed significant post-stimulation reductions in beta and gamma band activity. Prior rTMS work in AVH has reported network level modulation within temporoparietal language circuits alongside symptom change, including alterations in oscillatory dynamics and functional connectivity, supporting a distributed rather than purely focal effect ( Gornerova et al., 2023 ; Zhang et al., 2025 ). In this context, the BA40 beta/gamma decrease can be viewed as a downstream network signature of targeting the left auditory cortex/TPJ. Nonetheless, single-case EEG cannot establish mechanism; these find - ings should be considered convergent and hypothesis generating. While EEG findings converge with symptom change, such measures can vary independently of clinical response, and single case designs can not ascribe mechanism. The rapid onset of improvement — within the first session — appears atypical and may reflect expectancy/placebo effects, spontaneous fluctuation, or statedependent susceptibility of the targeted network. Symptom remission lasted for one month following treatment.

chunks.file_name: Provaznikova - 2026 - Remission of auditory verbal hallucinations following neuronavigated, personalized rTMS guided by EEG, MRI and auditory evoked potentials - a case report.pdf
chunks.title: Provaznikova 2026 Remission of auditory verbal hallucinations following neuronavigated, personalized rTMS guided by EEG, MRI and auditory evoked potentials a case report
chunks.text: y reflect expectancy/placebo effects, spontaneous fluctuation, or statedependent susceptibility of the targeted network. Symptom remission lasted for one month following treatment. This real world attempt at maintenance underscores that durability may depend on adherence, clinician support, and scheduling feasibility; while the patient ultimately rated AVH as ‘manageable, ’ structured, prospectively defined maintenance and adherence supports are likely important for sustaining remission. A pragmatic maintenance approach could include tapering schedules (e.g., weekly for 4 – 6 weeks, then biweekly, then monthly), fixedinterval boosters (e.g., 1 – 2 sessions every 2 – 4 weeks), or symptom-triggered boosters. Despite the chronic nature of AVH, no published protocols currently address maintenance rTMS for this condition. While rTMS can reduce symptom-related cortical activity temporarily, sustained effects may require repeated sessions.

chunks.file_name: Provaznikova - 2026 - Remission of auditory verbal hallucinations following neuronavigated, personalized rTMS guided by EEG, MRI and auditory evoked potentials - a case report.pdf
chunks.title: Provaznikova 2026 Remission of auditory verbal hallucinations following neuronavigated, personalized rTMS guided by EEG, MRI and auditory evoked potentials a case report
chunks.text: otocols currently address maintenance rTMS for this condition. While rTMS can reduce symptom-related cortical activity temporarily, sustained effects may require repeated sessions. Though AVH-specific data are limited, studies in depression suggest that main - tenance rTMS — especially when administered more than twice per month — can reduce relapse risk, with vulnerability peaking around five months post-treatment ( d ’ Andrea et al., 2023 ). EEG/AEP-guided targeting appears to be a practical, cost-effective bedside approach that offers millisecond temporal resolution linked to stimulus-locked auditory components (e.g., N100), thereby allowing physiology-informed, real-time targeting. In contrast, fMRI-guided TMS provides higher spatial precision and facilitates whole-brain network mapping, although its use may be constrained by access and cost ( SP Baliga and Mehta, 2021 ). A pragmatic approach is to use EEG/AEP when feasibility and speed are paramount, reserving fMRI for complex or re - fractory cases; hybrid EEG – fMRI workflows merit head to head evalu - ation with implementation and cost-effectiveness endpoints.

chunks.file_name: Provaznikova - 2026 - Remission of auditory verbal hallucinations following neuronavigated, personalized rTMS guided by EEG, MRI and auditory evoked potentials - a case report.pdf
chunks.title: Provaznikova 2026 Remission of auditory verbal hallucinations following neuronavigated, personalized rTMS guided by EEG, MRI and auditory evoked potentials a case report
chunks.text: are paramount, reserving fMRI for complex or re - fractory cases; hybrid EEG – fMRI workflows merit head to head evalu - ation with implementation and cost-effectiveness endpoints. Further research is needed to evaluate the efficacy, optimal param - eters, and maintenance strategies of this combined targeting approach. This case adds to the growing body of evidence suggesting that anatomically and functionally precise rTMS may improve outcomes for patients with treatment-resistant AVH, and it highlights the importance of continued innovation in neuromodulatory psychiatry. chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: Hospital Zurich, 8032 Zurich, Switzerland; 2 Department of Psychiatry, Hacettepe University Faculty of Medicine, 06230 Ankara, Turkey * To whom correspondence should be addressed: Judith Rohde, Department of Adult Psychiatry and Psychotherapy, Psychiatric Uni versity Hospital Zurich, Lenggstrasse 31, 8032 Zurich, Switzerland (judith.rohde@bli.uzh.ch ) Background and Hypothesis: Heart-rate variability (HRV) is a low-cost marker of autonomic regulation and a poten- tial prognostic biomarker in psychosis. We hypothesized that HRV marke rs recorded at admission are associated with remission at discharge in patients with suspected first- episode psychosis. Study Design: In this retrospective study, 78 inpatients (mean age: 30.8 years; 45% female) admitted to the Psychiatric University Hospital Zurich between 2018 and 2024 underwent a 2-min electrocardiogram on admission. Frequency-domainindices(log-transformedhigh-frequency [HF] and low-frequency [LF] power) and the linear slope of heart rate over time (beats per minute [BPM] slope) were extracted.

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: Frequency-domainindices(log-transformedhigh-frequency [HF] and low-frequency [LF] power) and the linear slope of heart rate over time (beats per minute [BPM] slope) were extracted. Remission status at discharge w as determined fromclinician-ratedscalesandclinicalevaluation.Binomial logistic regression with 5-fold stratified cross-validation assessed predictive accuracy (area under the receiver operating curve, AUC). Study Results: Twenty-six patients (33.3 %) remitted. Sex was not associated with outcome. In the initial model, log HF (P =.011) and BPM slope (P =.014) predicted remission,whereaslogLFandmeanBPMdidnot.Thefinal model retaining log HF and BPM slope achieved an A UC of 0.714. Lower log HF and more rapidly declining BPM slope at admission were linked to higher odds of remission. Conclusions: An HRV profile combining low vagally medi- ated HF power with rapid heart rate decrease predicted short-term remission, supporting the autonomic-flexibility framework.HRVmayaidearlytreatmentstratification,but findings r equire prospective validation with larger samples, respiratory monitoring, and standardized interventions. Key wor ds:heart rate variability; first-episode psychosis; remission prediction; autonomic nervous system; ECG biomark ers; physiological markers. Introduction Psychotic disorders are serious mental health conditions, with an estimated global point prevalence of 3.89 per 1000 individuals and a lifetime prevalence of 7.49 per 1000individuals,affectingmillionsworldwide. 1 Thesedis- orders, often begin in early adulthood, 2 are associated with high rates of psychiatric and somatic comorbidities,3 and carry an elevated risk of suicide.4 Among individuals receiving treatment following a first episode of psychosis, re mission rates are 58% and recovery rates 38%.5 Despite this, there remains a significant treatment gap, with only 29% of affected individuals receiving appropriate care.6 Several factors have been identified as predictors of symptomatic remission, including demographic factors (eg, female sex, treatment onset, or symptom severity7 ), and neurophysiological biomarkers derived from neu- roimaging studies8 or digital biomarkers.9 However, these approaches h

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: (eg, female sex, treatment onset, or symptom severity7 ), and neurophysiological biomarkers derived from neu- roimaging studies8 or digital biomarkers.9 However, these approaches have shown limited predictive value 10 and oftenrequirespecializedequipment, 9 limitingtheirutility for understanding of the biological mechanisms underly- ing relapse.11 Given the limitations of current biomarkers, an alternative approach to identifying predictive biomarkers couldbetheanalysisof heartratevariability(HRV).HRV describes fluctuations in time between consecutive heart- beatsandiswidelyrecognizedasanon-invasivemarkerof autonomicnervoussystemactivity.Itreflectsthedynamic interplay between sympathetic and parasympathetic influences on heart function, with higher HRV indicating betteradaptabilityandautonomicregulation,whilelower HRV is associated with autonomic dysfunction and potential health impairments.

chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: ces on heart function, with higher HRV indicating betteradaptabilityandautonomicregulation,whilelower HRV is associated with autonomic dysfunction and potential health impairments. 12 HRV can be assessed using multiple analytical approaches, including time- domain,frequency-domain,andnonlinearmethods,with electrocardiography (ECG) being the standard mea- 1 Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/schizophreniabulletin/advance-article/doi/10.1093/schbul/sbaf191/8305265 by Theologisches Seminar der Universität Zürich user on 30 October 2025

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: Schizophrenia Bulletin, 2025, Vol. 00, No. 00 surement tool. 12 Frequency-domain analysis estimates the distribution of heart rate power across 4 bands— ultra-low, very-low (VLF), low (LF), and high frequency (HF)—as defined by international guidelines.13 Psychiatric disorders, including schizophrenia, have been associated with autonomic dysregulation and alterations in HRV,14-17 and some evidence links HRV to symptom severity18 or changes following interventions such as electroconvulsive therapy.19 Nonetheless, HRV’s role as a predictor of short-term clinical outcomes in first-episode psychosis remains underexplored. Together with the marked heterogeneity in illness tra jectories and the large unmet clinical need, there is a pressing demand for low-cost, non-invasive tools to support personalized treatment planning and early prognostic decision-making. To address this gap, the present study investigates whether frequency-domain HRV parameters at hospi- tal admission are associated with remission status at discharge in individuals with suspected first-episode psychosis in a real-world setting.

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: uency-domain HRV parameters at hospi- tal admission are associated with remission status at discharge in individuals with suspected first-episode psychosis in a real-world setting. We hypothesize that HRV markers at admission will be associated with treatment outcomes, supporting their utility as physi- ological indicators for early prognosis in personalized psychosis care. Methods Study Design and Pa rticipants This retrospective observational study included 78 patients with suspected first-episode psychosis (ICD- 10: any F2 diagnosis) who were admitted to the Psy- chiatric University Hospital Zurich, Switzerland for routine inpatient treatment between 2018 and 2024. We analyzedpatientdata,includingECG,validatedclinician- administered assessments, and discharge letters. Procedure Upon admission, each patient underwent a simple bipo- lar ECG recording during resting state as part of rou- tine diagnostic evaluations. For HRV analysis, 120 s of artifact-free ECG da ta were used. Details of the HRV processing pipeline are provided below. Clinician-administered assessments included standard- ized and validated rating scales.

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: rtifact-free ECG da ta were used. Details of the HRV processing pipeline are provided below. Clinician-administered assessments included standard- ized and validated rating scales. These comprised the Clinical Global Impressions Scale—Severity (CGI-S), which evaluates the severity of mental illness on a 7- point scale (1=normal/not ill to 7=extremely ill),20 and the Health of the Nation Outcome Scales (HoNOS), a 12-item instrument assessing behavioral, cognitive, symp- tomatic, and social functioning problems on a scale from 0 (no problem) to 4 (severe problem), with a maximum total score of 48. 21 Based on these clinical assessments and additional validated psychometric instruments, a final clinical judgment regarding remission status was documented in the discharge letter, which served as the outcome definition in this study. Data Anal ysis ECG Data Analysis. HRV was computed using the DeepPsy Analysis Pipeline (version 1.1.7; DeepPSY, Zollikerberg, CH), 22 which follows the Kubios HRV standard analysis framework (https://www.kubios.com/). ECG peak positions were visually inspected to ensure accuracy before processing. RR intervals (time between consecutive R peaks;

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: ndard analysis framework (https://www.kubios.com/). ECG peak positions were visually inspected to ensure accuracy before processing. RR intervals (time between consecutive R peaks; ms) were derived from the verified peaks, interpolated using cubic splines, and detrended using a smoothness priors approach. Power spectral density was then estimated using Welch’s method. Standard HRV metrics were extracted, including VLF, LF, HF, LF/HF ratio, beats per minute (BPM), a ndBPM slope, calculated for both the full ECG trace and the first 120 s. Classical time-domain indices such as standard deviationof normal-to-normalintervals(SDNN)orroot mean square of successive differences (RMSSD) are not available in this certified software. Sample Char acteristics Descriptive analyses were conducted to characterize the sample’s basic demographic and clinical characteristics and to examine gender differences across variables, as observed previously.14 Initially, percentages, means, and SE were calculated for the total sample using SPSS (IBM SPSS Statistics, Version 30.0.0.0). Subsequently, gender- specific descriptive statistics were obtained.

To determine appropriate group comparisons, the nor- mality of each continuous variable was assessed using the Shapiro–Wilk test. Independent-samples t-tests were applied for normally distributed variables; Welch’s t-test was used when variances were unequal (Levene’s test). Non-normally distributed variables were compared using the Mann–Whitney U test, and group differences we re reported using mean ranks. Associations between gender and remission status were analyzed using chi-square tests and cross-tabulations. All 78 participants were included in the analyses w ith no missing data. Based on model performance and conceptual relevance, log(HF)andBPMslopewereretainedforthefinalmodel, which was likewise trained using 5-fold cross-validation and optimized for the area under the ROC curve (AUC). Out-of-fold predictions were used to compute a cross- validated ROC curve. A ll preprocessing steps, including variable transformation, handling of missing data, and recoding of remission status, were completed prior to model fitting. Ethics Approv al Participants provided written informed consent for the use of their data. As the study did not fall under the Swiss HumanResearchAct,formalapprovalfromtheCantonal Ethics Committee of Zurich was not required (BASEC No. Req-2020-00840). The study was not register ed in a clinical trial registry, as retrospective observational stud- ies are not subject to mandatory registration. Results Sample Char acteristics Our sample comprised 78 patients with a mean age of 30.79 years. Of these, 45% were female (n=35), and 55% were male (n=43).

Table 1 presents detailed demo- graphic, clinical, and ECG characteristics at admission. All patients received standard inpatient care, including regular consultations with a psychiatrist, at least 1 antipsychotic medication, and adjunctive therapies such as occupational therapy. At discharge, 33.3% of patients (n=26) were rated as being in remission, while 66.7% (n=52) showed partial or no remission. Among female patients, 28.6% (n=10) achieved remission compared to 37.2% (n=16) of male patients. No significant sex during hospitalization, all patients received atypical antipsychotic medication. In the partial/non-remission group (n=52), 39 (75%) were discharged with a single antipsychotic, 8 (15%) with more than 1 antipsychotic, and 5 patients (10%) without an antipsychotic. The most frequently prescribed drug was olanzapine (23%), followed by risperidone (19%) and aripiprazole (15%).

In the remission group (n =26), 22 patients (85%) received 1 antipsychotic, 1 (4%) more than 1 antipsychotic, and 3 patients (12%) no antipsychotic. The most fre- quently prescribed drug was again olanzapine (27%), followed by risperidone (23%) and aripiprazole (15%). All antipsychotic prescriptions were documented as clinically adequate doses and durations at discharge. There was no significant difference between groups in the distribution of patients receiving no, one, or more than one antipsychotic medication (χ2 test, P >.05). Of the 78 patients included in the study, at discharge, 21 (27%) were diagnosed with schizophrenia (ICD-10: F20.x), 32 (41%) with acute and transient psychotic dis- orders (F23.x), and 3 (4%) with schizoaffective disorder (F25.x). The remaining 22 (28%) pat ientswerediagnosed with other psychiatric disorders, such as affective or sub- stance use disorders. Prediction of remission.

An initial model using binomial logistic regression included all 4 ECG parameters (log(HF), log(LF), BPM, and BPM slope). Table 2 presents the coefficients of the initial logistic regression model with all 4 ECG-based predictors. Among these, log(HF) and BPM slope w ere significantly associated with remission status (P<.05), while the other predictors did not reach statistical significance. Based on preliminary model performance and concep- tual relevance, log(HF) and BPM slope were retained for the final prediction model, which achieved an AUC of 0.714 (Figure 1 ). Table 3 shows the coefficients of the final binomialregressionmodel. Figure 2 illustratesBPM slopeandlog(HF)forno/partialremissionandremission. Discussion Summary of Results and Interpretation The present study examined whether HRV parameters assessed at hospital admission could predict remission status at discharge in patients with suspected first-episode psychosis.

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: ion The present study examined whether HRV parameters assessed at hospital admission could predict remission status at discharge in patients with suspected first-episode psychosis. Among the 78 patients included, one-third (33.3%) were classified as being in remission at discharge. No significant sex differences were observed in remission rates or in clinical and physiological measures. In the ini- tial binomial logistic regression model including 4 ECG- derived predictors, log-transformed HF power and the linear change of heart rate over time (BPM slope) were significantly associated with remission status, whereas log(LF) and BPM were not. These findings led to a final model that retained log(HF) and BPM slope as predic- tors, yielding an AUC of 0.714 based on 5-fold stratified cross-validation. In other words, patients with lower HF power and faster decreasing heart rate at admission had higher odds of remission. Beyond first-episode psychosis, baseline HRV has also been studied as a predictor of treatment response in other psychiatric disorders.

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: e at admission had higher odds of remission. Beyond first-episode psychosis, baseline HRV has also been studied as a predictor of treatment response in other psychiatric disorders. Across depression, anxiety disorders,PTSD,andautism,similar indices(particularly HF power, and in some studies other indices, such as RMSSD and SDNN) have been examined.23-28 However, findings are heterogeneous, and recent reviews emphasize that predictive effects vary by diagnosis, treatment modality, subgroup characteristics, and the specific HRV metric.29 ,30 In psychosis, predictive work remains scarce and differs from the present study in both HRV param- eteriza tion and outcomes. Violin Plots of BPM Slope (Left) and Log-Transformed High-Frequency Power (log(HF); Right) by Remission Status Based Differences in Remission Rates Were Observed (χ2 test, P >.05) shannon entropy) to antipsychotic side effects and treatment adherence,31 or examined changes in nonlinear complexity (ApEn/SampEn) in relation to clozapine response in treatment-resistant schizophrenia; notably, baseline differences did not predict response in the latter, whereas week-8 complexity values and their increase over time were associated with better outcomes.32 In contrast, our model targets first-episode psychosis and combines a frequency-domain vagal index (HF) with a short-term heart-rate settling measure (BPM slope) at admission to predict symptomatic remission, a dynamic feature not included in previous psychosis studies.

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: al index (HF) with a short-term heart-rate settling measure (BPM slope) at admission to predict symptomatic remission, a dynamic feature not included in previous psychosis studies. Our findings suggest that specific HRV features— namely, lower HF, which reflects reduced parasympa- thetic activity and increased physiological tension,12 ,16 ,33 and a more rapidly declining BPM slope, which may reflect greater autonomic relaxation ability—could serve as early indicators of clinical improvement during treat- ment of first psychotic episodes. While BPM slope is not a standar d HRV parameter, a less steep or negative slope over the 2-min ECG may reflect increased capacity for autonomic downregulation in a neutral clinical setting. HRV is widely regarded as a peripheral window into central autonomic network. From an allostatic perspective,34 the cardiovascular system must flexibly allocate metabolic resources to maintain internal stability when confronted with physiological or psychological challenges.

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: c perspective,34 the cardiovascular system must flexibly allocate metabolic resources to maintain internal stability when confronted with physiological or psychological challenges. Low overall HRV is generally interpreted as a sign of reduced adaptive capacity and heightened allostatic load.35 ,36 However, the pattern of sympathetic- parasympatheticbalancematters:HFreflectsrespiratory- linked vagal modulation.37 Although higher HF is usually considered adaptive, some have argued that excessive vagal tone may become maladaptive under chronic stress 38 leading to a rigid, energy-conserving state (autonomic fatigue 39 ). In that context, our find- ing that lower HF predicted remission suggests the absence of a parasympathetic dominance at treatment entry, potentially denoting a more adaptive autonomic set-point.40 Yet, empirical evidence for such “excessive vagal dominance” is limited and mixed, so this interpre- tation should be viewed with care. Another explanation could be that patients during an acute psychotic episode often show increased physiological tension, reflected in low p arasympathetic and high sympathetic tone.

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: nother explanation could be that patients during an acute psychotic episode often show increased physiological tension, reflected in low p arasympathetic and high sympathetic tone. The likelihood of these patients to benefit from first-line treatment, including antipsychotic medication and the safe, structured atmosphere of an inpatient ward, might be higher than in those who already display higher levels of parasympathetic tone and relaxation at admission. This interpretation is supported by previous literature suggestingthatschizophreniaisassociatedwithparasym- pathetic dysfunction, particularly in early psychosis.41 On the other hand, the more rapidly declining BPM slope in remitters across the short tracing indicates temporal adaptation: a decrease trend implies rapid settling into baseline once external stressors diminish. Such adaption fits the concept of autonomic flexibility— the capacity to shift effectively between sympathetic mobilization and parasympathetic recovery aligning with the neurovisceral integration model.

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: concept of autonomic flexibility— the capacity to shift effectively between sympathetic mobilization and parasympathetic recovery aligning with the neurovisceral integration model. 33 ,42 ,43 This model posits that prefrontal cortical hubs orchestrate rapid, context-appropriate adjustments of autonomic output via cortico-limbic-brainstem loops. 42 Higher HRV— particularly dynamic shifts, not static levels—indexes the integrity of these loops. In our data, patients who eventually remitted displayed (i) absence of parasympathetic over-dominance (lower HF) and (ii) fast settling of heart rate, potentially consistent with intact top-down control during a state of increased tension (psychosis). Taken together, the combi- nation of relatively low HF and intact heart-rate recovery may reflect a balanced yet flexible autonomic baseline. This pattern is in line with recent recommendations to considermultipleHRVparametersincombinationrather than in isolation. A recent umbrella review emphasized 5 Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/schizophreniabulletin/advance-article/doi/10.1093/schbul/sbaf191/8305265 by Theologisches Seminar der Universität Zürich user on 30 October 2025

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: Schizophrenia Bulletin, 2025, Vol. 00, No. 00 thatcombiningindicessuchasHFandRMSSDimproves clinical relevance and specificity across psychiatric pop- ulations.44 Although global HRV was lower, patients in remission showed a more balanced sympatho-vagal tone, without global sympathetic nervous system over activity, which can be interpreted as a physiologically resilient state.45 In line with this interpretation, patients who did not go into remission tended to show higher HF and/or less rapidly declining BPM slope patterns. These patterns may indicate a parasympathetic dominance wherein the system might be “locked” in an energy conservation mode,36 but lacks the flexibility to re-engage sympathetic drive when new challenges arise. I n psychosis, such rigiditycouldinterferewithengagementwithpsychother- apeutic input, behavioral activation, or medication adherence and drug effectiveness, perpetuating symptom burden. At first glance, this pattern may appear counterintu- itive, since higher HRV is often viewed as a marker of healthier autonomic regulation.

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: ctiveness, perpetuating symptom burden. At first glance, this pattern may appear counterintu- itive, since higher HRV is often viewed as a marker of healthier autonomic regulation. However, accumulating evidence suggests that the prognostic role of baseline HRVisnotuniversallylinear,butdependsonclinicalsub- groupandtreatmentcontext.Forexample,inrandomized trials of CBT for anxiety disorders, lower baseline HRV predictedsuperioroutcomes.25 Arecentsystematicreview of anxiety disorders also highlighted mixed findings, with low HRV sometimes linked to better, but in other cases to poorer, treatment response.29 Moreover, in general- ized anxiety disorder, baseline HF moderated treatment effects:

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: , with low HRV sometimes linked to better, but in other cases to poorer, treatment response.29 Moreover, in general- ized anxiety disorder, baseline HF moderated treatment effects: patients with low HF improved more with flu- oxetine than with mindfulness-based therapy, whereas this difference disappeared in those with high HF.26 Sim- ilar observations of low b aseline HF predicting better outcomes have been reported in depression (esketamine treatment)27 and in autism (response to propranolol).28 Together, these findings support that HRV’s prognostic meaning is context-dependent and may, in first-episode psychosis, reflect an acute, plasticity-related imbalance rather than a simple deficit. Insummary,whilepreviousstudieshavelinkedreduced HRV to increased symptom severity,18 ,19 our results point to a potential prognostic role of HRV—beyond symptom correlation—at the onset of treatment. This apparent discrepancy may point to a state–trait distinction:

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: ,19 our results point to a potential prognostic role of HRV—beyond symptom correlation—at the onset of treatment. This apparent discrepancy may point to a state–trait distinction: lower HF at admission could reflect an acute, state-related autonomic dysregulation that is amenable to treatment, whereas persistently reduced vagal activity in chronic psychosis might represent a more stable trait marker of unfavorable prognosis. This distinction may reflect different physiological mechanisms underlying symptom burden versus tr eatment responsiveness, and aligns with the broader view of HRV as a transdiagnostic indicator of regulatory capacity rather than a disorder-specific biomarker,46 with potential implications for early clinical decision-making and personalized care. Limitations and Str engths This study has several limitations. First, the findings are preliminary in nature and should be interpreted with caution due to the exploratory design. Given the retro- spective, observational nature of the study, all findings are associational; the design does not permit causal inference regarding treatment effects.

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: atory design. Given the retro- spective, observational nature of the study, all findings are associational; the design does not permit causal inference regarding treatment effects. Second, no detailed information was available regarding the exact medication regimen at the time of ECG assessment at baseline. At thispoint,autonomicalterationslikelyreflectbothpatho- physiology and potential medication effects, although in this first-episode psychosis cohort, substantial antipsy- chotic exposure was unlikely. Importantly, this combined influence may itself carry prognostic value for subsequent remission. While most evidence points to reduced vagal HRV with antipsychotic treatment, findings have been inconsistent, with some studies reporting no signifi- cant medication-related effects. This inconsistency may reflect differences in sample characteristics, antipsychotic agents, dosages, and methodological approaches.32 ,44 ,47 In addition, further factors may influence HRV values and should be considered when interpreting these results.

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: ipsychotic agents, dosages, and methodological approaches.32 ,44 ,47 In addition, further factors may influence HRV values and should be considered when interpreting these results. For instance, females typically show lower time-domain HRV and lower LF/HF ratios than males,14 although we did not observe sex-related differences in this sample. Additionally, certain cardiac conditions, such as arrhyth- mias, can artificially inflate HRV values, potentially con- founding their interpretation.48 Third, treatment followed a naturalistic “therapy-as-usual” approach, based on the clinicians’ discretion, without standardization of pharmacological or psychosocial interventions, and with diagnosticheterogeneitytypicalof early-episodereferrals. Furthermore, widely used research scales such as PANSS, SANS, or SAPS were not systematically collected in this naturalistic setting. Accordingly, remission status was determined based on CGI-S, HoNOS, and clinician ratings, complemented by additional instruments where available. Prior work indicates that CGI ratings correlate strongly with PANSS scores, 49 supporting the validity of this approach.

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: gs, complemented by additional instruments where available. Prior work indicates that CGI ratings correlate strongly with PANSS scores, 49 supporting the validity of this approach. Moreover, we did not account for other therapeutic or contextual factors that may have influenced outcomes, such as psychosocial interventions, social support, or length of inpatient stay. Fourth, ECG recording covered only a 2-min period, which is less than the suggested 5-min period for short-term recordings.50 Lastly,noECGwasperformedatdischarge, which limited conclusions about potential changes in autonomic function and their relation to clinical improvement. In addition, respiratory parameters were not recorded, limiting the interpretability of frequency- domain measures—particularly high-frequency power, which is strongly influenced by breathing patterns.51 ,52 In addition, the software used for HRV analysis did not provide classical time-domain indices such as SDNN or RMSSD, which may have added complementary information on autonomic regulation.

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: Schizophrenia Bulletin, 2025, Vol. 00, No. 00 This study offers a first step toward evaluating the pre- dictive utility of ECG-derived parameters in first-episode psychosis. The use of non-invasive, cost-effective, and easily applicable physiological mar kers such as HRV is particularly promising for translation into routine care. Clinical and Methodological Implications Although AUC values between 0.7 and 0.8 are frequently labeled as indicating “moderate”or “acceptable”discrim- ination, such qualitative classifications vary substantially acrossstudiesandlackconsistentempiricaljustification. 53 Considering this, we report the AUC descriptively with- outapplyingaqualitativelabel.Importantly,evenmodest predictiveaccuracymayhav eclinicalvaluewhenbasedon simple, low-cost, and scalable measures. These exploratory findings provide clinically relevant insights and may inform future approaches to early treatmentstratificationinfirst-episodepsychosis.Yet,our study did not include additional ECG-derived markers or respiratory recordings, or ECG at discharge, which may have provided complementary information.

chunks.file_name: Rohde, Weber - 2025 - Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success - HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis.pdf
chunks.title: Rohde, Weber 2025 Autonomic Flexibility and Early Treatment Success HRV predicts remission in first episode psychosis
chunks.text: rst-episodepsychosis.Yet,our study did not include additional ECG-derived markers or respiratory recordings, or ECG at discharge, which may have provided complementary information. Outlook and Future Directions Taken together, this study provides preliminary evidence thatECG-basedHRVmeasuresmaysupportearlyidenti- fication of patients with favorable treatment trajectories. Namely, lower HF power and a more rapidly declin- ing BPM slope at admission—markers of balanced yet flexible autonomic control—were associated with short- term remission after a first psychotic episode, under- scoring the importance of autonomic flexibility rather than absolute vagal tone. Future prospective longitudinal studies with larger samples, repeated HRV and physiolog- icalassessments—includingrespiratorymonitoring—and standardized treatment protocols are needed to validate and refine these predictive models and to evaluate treat- menteffectsovertime.Additionally,futurestudiesshould include both time- and frequency-domain measures to provide a more comprehensive assessment of autonomic function in first-episode psychosis.

Ideally, such stud- ies will also examine the integration of HRV features with clinical, behavioral, or digital phenotyping data to improve prediction accuracy and practical utility. BOLD signal variability as potential new biomarker of functional neurological disorders Ayla Schneider a , b , 1 , Samantha Weber a , b , c , 1 , Anna Wyss a , d , Serafeim Loukas a , e , Selma Aybek a , f , * a Department of Neurology, Psychosomatic Medicine Unit, Inselspital Bern University Hospital, University of Bern, 3012 Bern, Switzerland b Translational Imaging Center (TIC), Swiss Institute for Translational and Entrepreneurial Medicine, 3010 Bern, Switzerland c University of Zurich, Psychiatric University Hospital Zurich, Department of Psychiatry, Psychotherapy and Psychosomatics, 8032 Zurich, Switzerland d Graduate School for Health Sciences (GHS), University of Bern, 3006 Bern, Switzerland e Institute of Bioengineering, Ecole Polytechnique F ´ed ´erale de Lausanne (EPFL), 1015 Lausanne, Switzerland f Faculty of Science and Medicine, University of Fribourg, 1700 Fribourg, Switzerland ARTICLE INFO Keywords:

chunks.file_name: Schneider & Weber - 2024 - BOLD signal variability.pdf
chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: lytechnique F ´ed ´erale de Lausanne (EPFL), 1015 Lausanne, Switzerland f Faculty of Science and Medicine, University of Fribourg, 1700 Fribourg, Switzerland ARTICLE INFO Keywords: Conversion disorders Longitudinal Prognostic Biomarker Insula Supplementary motor area ABSTRACT Background: Functional neurological disorder (FND) is a common neuropsychiatric condition with established diagnostic criteria and effective treatments but for which the underlying neuropathophysiological mechanisms remain incompletely understood. Recent neuroimaging studies have revealed FND as a multi-network brain disorder, unveiling alterations across limbic, self-agency, attentional/salience, and sensorimotor networks. However, the relationship between identified brain alterations and disease progression or improvement is less explored. Methods: This study included resting-state functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) data from 79 patients with FND and 74 age and sex-matched healthy controls (HC). First, voxel-wise BOLD signal variability was computed for each participant and the group-wise difference was calculated.

chunks.file_name: Schneider & Weber - 2024 - BOLD signal variability.pdf
chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: with FND and 74 age and sex-matched healthy controls (HC). First, voxel-wise BOLD signal variability was computed for each participant and the group-wise difference was calculated. Second, we investigated the po- tential of BOLD signal variability to serve as a prognostic biomarker for clinical outcome in 47 patients who attended a follow-up measurement after eight months. Results: The results demonstrated higher BOLD signal variability in key networks, including the somatomotor, salience, limbic, and dorsal attention networks, in patients compared to controls. Longitudinal analysis revealed an increase in BOLD signal variability in the supplementary motor area (SMA) in FND patients who had an improved clinical outcome, suggesting SMA variability as a potential state biomarker. Additionally, higher BOLD signal variability in the left insula at baseline predicted a worse clinical outcome. Conclusion: This study contributes to the understanding of FND pathophysiology, emphasizing the dynamic nature of neural activity and highlighting the potential of BOLD signal variability as a valuable research tool.

chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: o the understanding of FND pathophysiology, emphasizing the dynamic nature of neural activity and highlighting the potential of BOLD signal variability as a valuable research tool. The insula and SMA emerge as promising regions for further investigation as prognostic and state markers. 1. Introduction Functional neurological disorder (FND) is a common medical con- dition ( Bennett et al., 2021; Carson and Lehn, 2016 ; Hallett et al., 2022 ) presenting with diverse neurological symptoms and typically motor or sensory symptom patterns that cannot been explained by an underlying classical neurological disorder ( Aybek and Perez, 2022; Espay et al., 2018a ). Currently there are well established rule-in diagnostic criteria ( American Psychiatric Association, 2022 ; Varley et al., 2023 ) and multimodal treatment options available ( Bennett et al., 2021 ; Hallett et al., 2022 ; LaFaver, 2020; Varley et al., 2023 ), but our understanding of the neuropathophysiological mechanisms, the underlying develop- ment and clinical course of the diverse symptoms remains limited ( Drane et al., 2021 ).

chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: ), but our understanding of the neuropathophysiological mechanisms, the underlying develop- ment and clinical course of the diverse symptoms remains limited ( Drane et al., 2021 ). Using diverse neuroimaging techniques, recent research has unveiled FND as a multi-network brain disorder ( Drane et al., 2021 ; Perez et al., 2021 ). This condition has been characterized by alterations in task- based as well as in resting-state analysis across limbic/salience * Corresponding author at: Faculty of Sciences and Medicine, University of Fribourg, Office 2.106d, Chemin du Mus ´ee 5, 1700 Fribourg, Switzerland. chunks.text: NeuroImage: Clinical 43 (2024) 103625 2 ( Demartini et al., 2021; Hassa et al., 2021, 2017; Stone et al., 2007; Voon et al., 2011; Weber et al., 2024 ), self-agency ( Baek et al., 2017; Maurer et al., 2016; Voon et al., 2010 ), attentional ( Marapin et al., 2020; Stager et al., 2022 ) and sensorimotor networks ( Aybek et al., 2015 ; Voon et al., 2011; Weber et al., 2022b ). Disturbed self-agency ( Baek et al., 2017; Marapin et al., 2020 ) in FND patients seems to be manifested in a hypoactivation of the right temporal parietal junction (TPJ) and decreased connectivity of the TPJ with limbic and sensorimotor regions in task-based ( Voon et al., 2010 ) as well as resting-state ( Maurer et al., 2016 ) studies that could potentially be reversed by neuromodulation ( Bühler et al., 2024 ). These findings go align with a lower sense of control in a game manipulated agency in children with functional seizure where also a poorer selective attention and cognitive inhibition was reported ( Stager et al., 2022 ).

chunks.text: e of control in a game manipulated agency in children with functional seizure where also a poorer selective attention and cognitive inhibition was reported ( Stager et al., 2022 ). In the limbic network, hyper- activation of the amygdala while exposed to fearful emotional stimuli ( Voon et al., 2010 ) as well as during emotional stimulation with simultaneously passive movement of the affected hand ( Hassa et al., 2017 ) and during cognitive reappraisal ( Hassa et al., 2021 ) was re- ported. In an earlier fMRI study patients showed hyperactivation of the left insula while trying to move their affected limb compared to HC who simulated the weakness ( Stone et al., 2007 ). These findings go align with the reporting of higher right amygdala and left anterior insula activa- tions during internally and externally generated movements ( Voon et al., 2011 ). In the same study lower activation of the right supple- mentary motor cortex (SMA) was revealed ( Voon et al., 2011 ). In contrast a higher activation of the SMA during negative emotions in FND patients were reported indicating that there might be a link between emotions and motor dysfunctions ( Aybek et al., 2015 ).

chunks.file_name: Schneider & Weber - 2024 - BOLD signal variability.pdf
chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: r activation of the SMA during negative emotions in FND patients were reported indicating that there might be a link between emotions and motor dysfunctions ( Aybek et al., 2015 ). This limbic- motor interaction was also shown in the form of increased connectivity in a resting-state analysis between cingulo-insular networks and motor control areas which showed a correlation with symptom severity ( Diez et al., 2019; Li et al., 2015a, 2015b ). Likewise, in our previous study on the same cohort, we detected altered resting-state insular co-activation patterns with the somatomotor- and default mode networks (DMN), which was associated with duration of illness ( Weber et al., 2024 ). Despite these tremendous advances in the understanding of the role of these brain regions and network connectivity in FND, there is a lack of knowledge on the links between the identified alterations in brain ac- tivity and the clinical outcome � the progression or amelioration of the disorder.

chunks.file_name: Schneider & Weber - 2024 - BOLD signal variability.pdf
chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: in FND, there is a lack of knowledge on the links between the identified alterations in brain ac- tivity and the clinical outcome � the progression or amelioration of the disorder. In a recent positron emission tomography (PET) study, the resting state metabolism of left and right subgenual anterior cingular cortex at inclusion was negatively correlated with improvement of motor symptoms after three months, suggesting this could represent a metabolic state marker ( Conejero et al., 2022 ). Similarly, patients with functional tremor presented not only with a significant improvement in tremor severity but also with a decreased activation in the anterior/ paracingulate cortex during a basic-emotion task after 12 weeks of cognitive behavioural therapy ( Espay et al., 2019 ). Furthermore, a study in functional movement disorders patients undergoing a one-week multidisciplinary motor retraining treatment program identified greater primary motor cortex activation at baseline when the patients responded to the therapy program, together with a shift in amygdalar functional connectivity from motor regions towards prefrontal regions ( Faul et al., 2020 ).

chunks.file_name: Schneider & Weber - 2024 - BOLD signal variability.pdf
chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: when the patients responded to the therapy program, together with a shift in amygdalar functional connectivity from motor regions towards prefrontal regions ( Faul et al., 2020 ). These studies not only underscore that functional alterations in the brain might revert but can also be detected in response to a clinical improvement. Understanding how brain alterations evolve over time and parallels clinical course could bring valuable information into mechanisms but may also ultimately serve clinical purpose in identifying prognostic biomarkers. Within-group studies are important, as they can account for the coincidence of other neuropsychiatric dis- eases ( Perez et al., 2021 ) and longitudinal studies are needed to help disentangle between state and trait biomarkers ( Conejero et al., 2022 ; Perez et al., 2021 ). Until now, functional alterations in FND were mostly studied using the averaged time-course of blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) sig- nals. However, even the resting brain is highly variable in its activity and constantly adapting to its internal and external environment ( Brembs, 2021 ).

chunks.file_name: Schneider & Weber - 2024 - BOLD signal variability.pdf
chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: el-dependent (BOLD) sig- nals. However, even the resting brain is highly variable in its activity and constantly adapting to its internal and external environment ( Brembs, 2021 ). While in the past particularly this variability of the BOLD signals was considered as noise, it has attracted the attention of researchers in recent years as a more dynamic measurement of brain activity ( Garrett et al., 2011 ). This variability in the BOLD signal is suggested to represent an important feature of proper brain function ( Baracchini et al., 2021; Garrett et al., 2018, 2013 ) where an optimal variability encompasses a balance that provides enough stability but also the necessary flexibility for the execution of brain functionality ( Armbruster-Genç et al., 2016 ). Therefore, this more dynamic measurement is an option to evaluate the longitudinal changes in brain functioning, which has never been explored in FND so far. For example, motor recovery after stroke showed a correlation with altered temporal variability of the ipsilateral pre- central gyrus ( Hu et al., 2018 ).

chunks.file_name: Schneider & Weber - 2024 - BOLD signal variability.pdf
chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: n explored in FND so far. For example, motor recovery after stroke showed a correlation with altered temporal variability of the ipsilateral pre- central gyrus ( Hu et al., 2018 ). In other neuropsychiatric conditions, there is recent proof of such alterations in brain variability across diverse regions ( Kebets et al., 2021; Li et al., 2019; Wei et al., 2023; Zanella et al., 2022 ): For example, a reduction in BOLD signal variability was associated with improved emotion regulation in with attention/deficit/ hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), borderline personality disorder, or bi- polar disorder ( Kebets et al., 2021; Zanella et al., 2022 ). Also, patients ADHD presented with overall increased resting-state BOLD signal vari- ability in the prefrontal cortex ( Nomi et al., 2018 ), as well as the sensorimotor- and salience networks ( Kebets et al., 2021 ). Likewise, higher BOLD variability in schizophrenia across diverse brain networks were positively correlated to severity of symptoms ( Wei et al., 2023 ). Moreover.

chunks.file_name: Schneider & Weber - 2024 - BOLD signal variability.pdf
chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: et al., 2021 ). Likewise, higher BOLD variability in schizophrenia across diverse brain networks were positively correlated to severity of symptoms ( Wei et al., 2023 ). Moreover. In this exploratory study, by first examining alterations in BOLD signal variability between FND patients and healthy controls (HC) we aimed to uncover potential signatures of disrupted neural dynamics that may be associated with FND (cross-sectionally) hypothesizing to identify increased BOLD signal variability in diverse brain networks previously associated to FND pathology. Second, we investigated alterations in BOLD signal variability in FND patients over time (longitudinally) to examine alterations in BOLD signal variability with regards to clinical course, hypothesizing that an improvement in symptom severity might align with a reduction in BOLD signal variability in distinct brain re- gions. Overall, this study aims at identifying both state and prognostic factors of FND. 2. Methods 2.1.

chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: ght align with a reduction in BOLD signal variability in distinct brain re- gions. Overall, this study aims at identifying both state and prognostic factors of FND. 2. Methods 2.1. Participants 86 Patients with functional neurological disorder (FND) and 76 healthy controls (HC) participated in a resting-state fMRI study between june 2020 and february 2022, where clinical data were also acquired. Cross-sectional structural and functional imaging data of this cohort (FND patients and HC) have previously been published ( Weber et al., 2024, 2022a ). FND patients were recruited through the outpatient clinic of the department of Neurology, Inselspital, Bern University Hospital, switzerland. The FND diagnosis was established by a certified neurolo- gist according to DSM-5 criteria ( American Psychiatric Association, 2022 ) and ICD-10 ( World Health Organization, 2004 ) of FND (motor FND (F44.4), non-epileptic attack FND (F44.5), sensory FND (44.6) or mixed FND (44.7)) and using positive signs ( Stone et al., 2011 ). Mixed FND was only diagnosed if the patients presented with all three symp- tom types (motor, sensory and non-epileptic attacks).

chunks.file_name: Schneider & Weber - 2024 - BOLD signal variability.pdf
chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: ) and using positive signs ( Stone et al., 2011 ). Mixed FND was only diagnosed if the patients presented with all three symp- tom types (motor, sensory and non-epileptic attacks). HC comparable in age and sex were recruited through public advertisement. Inclusion criteria for patients included a diagnosis of FND and an age of minimum 16 years. Exclusion criteria for both groups included: Persons suffering from epilepsy, psychosis, severe major depressive disorder, or alcohol/ drug abuse (based on clinical documentation); previously brain surgery; implanted medical devices (pacemaker, infusion pumps); metallic foreign bodies in the head region except braces or dental fillings; breast- feeding or pregnant women; women with the intention to get pregnant; impaired understanding of the task due to language or cognitive A. Schneider et al.

chunks.file_name: Schneider & Weber - 2024 - BOLD signal variability.pdf
chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: NeuroImage: Clinical 43 (2024) 103625 3 difficulties. Board-certified neurologists performed the screening for psychosis, major depressive disorder, and/or drug abuse. Regardless of their clinical status, the patients were invited for a follow-up examina- tion after eight months, at which 53 patients followed this invitation. The reasons for the dropouts are shown in the flowchart in the Supple- mentary figure S1 . The study was approved by the local ethics com- mittee of the canton Bern (SNCTP000002289) and conducted according to the declaration of helsinki. 2.2. Clinical assessment At inclusion (T1), all participants completed the Beck ’ s Depression Inventory (BDI ( Beck et al., 1961 )) and the State-Trait Anxiety Inventory (STAI-S and STAI-T ( Spielberger et al., 1983 )) questionnaires. The State form (STAI-S) assesses the intensity of anxiety experienced by the participant during the test, in the recent past, or anticipates their feel- ings in a hypothetical scenario whereas the Trait form (STAI-T) looks at individual variations in anxiety predisposition and overall anxiety levels, providing insight into a person ’ s enduring anxious tendencies ( Spielberger et al., 1983 ).

chunks.file_name: Schneider & Weber - 2024 - BOLD signal variability.pdf
chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: ) looks at individual variations in anxiety predisposition and overall anxiety levels, providing insight into a person ’ s enduring anxious tendencies ( Spielberger et al., 1983 ). FND patients additionally underwent a clinical examination: Severity of illness was assessed by the Clinical Global Impressions Scale (CGI-I) from 1 to 7 (1 = normal, 7 = among the most ill patients ( Busner and Targum, 2007 )). In addition, for motor FND symptoms we used the Simplified Functional Movement Disorders Rating scale (S-FMDRS ( Nielsen et al., 2017 )). At follow-up (T2), we repeated CGI-I and S- FMDRS and additionally assessed the Clinical Global Improvement Score (CGI-II) from 1 to 7 (1 = very much improved, 7 = very much worse). Moreover, we assessed the type of therapy patients engaged in during the four weeks before joining the follow-up measurement. 2.3. Image acquisition MRI data were acquired using a 3 T scanner (Magnetom Prisma, Siemens, Germany). To reduce head movement, the head was fixed using foam cushions. Functional data were acquired using a gradient- echo planar imaging (EPI) sequence with the following parameters:

chunks.file_name: Schneider & Weber - 2024 - BOLD signal variability.pdf
chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: . To reduce head movement, the head was fixed using foam cushions. Functional data were acquired using a gradient- echo planar imaging (EPI) sequence with the following parameters: TR = 1.3 s, TE = 37 ms, flip angle = 52 ◦ , slice thickness = 2.2 mm, REF voxel size = 2.2x2.2x2.2 mm, TA = 6.39 min and 300 volumes. During the acquisition of the resting-state fMRI, the participants were instructed to lay as calm as possible, to stay awake, to fixate on a cross shown on the screen, and to not think of anything in particular. Structural data were acquired using a T1-weighted MPRAGE sequence with the following parameters: TR = 2.33 s, TE = 3.03 ms, flip angle = 8 ◦ , FoV read = 256 mm, 1 mm slice thickness and REF voxel size = 1.0x1.0x1.0 mm, TA = 5.27 min. 2.4. Resting-state preprocessing Preprocessing was performed using Statistical Parametric Mapping version 12 (SPM12; https://www.fil.ion.ucl.ac.uk/spm/software/ spm12/ ). Functional data were corrected for b0-field distortions. The images were realigned and then co-registered to the anatomical image.

chunks.file_name: Schneider & Weber - 2024 - BOLD signal variability.pdf
chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: s://www.fil.ion.ucl.ac.uk/spm/software/ spm12/ ). Functional data were corrected for b0-field distortions. The images were realigned and then co-registered to the anatomical image. After this, data was linearly detrended and further denoised using white matter and cerebrospinal fluid signals as well as movement parameters as regressors (including constant, linear, and quadratic trends, average white matter/cerebrospinal fluid time courses, translational and rota- tional motion time courses upon realignment). Images were normalized to a MNI (Montreal Neurological Institute) template, resampled to 3.0x3.0x3.0 mm and smoothed with a 6 mm FWHM Gaussian kernel. Signals were filtered using a bandpass filter between 0.01 and 0.1 Hz. Functional images were inspected for too high motion artefacts based on Power ’ s framewise displacement (FD) criterion at a threshold of FD > 0.5 mm ( Power et al., 2014 ). 2.5. Differences in BOLD signal variability (SD BOLD ) between FND and HC As SD BOLD might represent an adjunctive measure of brain dysfunc- tion in diverse neuropsychiatric disorders ( Kebets et al., 2021; Li et al., 2019; Wei et al., 2023;

chunks.file_name: Schneider & Weber - 2024 - BOLD signal variability.pdf
chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: n FND and HC As SD BOLD might represent an adjunctive measure of brain dysfunc- tion in diverse neuropsychiatric disorders ( Kebets et al., 2021; Li et al., 2019; Wei et al., 2023; Zanella et al., 2022 ), we investigated alterations in SD BOLD in FND patients compared to HC on a whole-brain level. Timeseries were mean centred aiming to eliminate the effect of the general activation of the voxels/regions in resting-state ( Baracchini et al., 2021 ). SD BOLD was determined as the voxel-wise standard devi- ation of the temporal BOLD signal (SD BOLD ) of each subject, resulting in a 3-dimensional SD BOLD map per subject, as also previously described in ( Kebets et al., 2021 ). To evaluate differences in the BOLD signal variability between FND and HC a voxel-wise t -test was performed using age and gender as var- iables of no interest. To correct for multiple comparisons, a family-wise error correction (FWE, P < 0.01) was applied at the cluster level. The analysis was repeated using the BDI and STAI-S as additional covariates of no-interest to correct for the effect of depression and anxiety, details are shown in Supplementary Material . 2.6.

chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: as repeated using the BDI and STAI-S as additional covariates of no-interest to correct for the effect of depression and anxiety, details are shown in Supplementary Material . 2.6. Longitudinal analysis In a secondary analysis, we investigated on a potential relationship between clinical outcome and SD BOLD in those clusters that were found significantly different between patients and HC. Therefore, we first quantified the results on a network-level by overlaying the significant clusters with the YEO network atlas ( Thomas Yeo et al., 2011 ). For the two most overlapping networks, a region of interest (ROI) was selected based on the AAL2-atlas ( Rolls et al., 2015 ). The average SD BOLD of the voxels in the selected AAL2-regions were extracted from the scans per- formed at T1 and T2, but only including those voxels from the AAL mask that overlapped with the initial clusters, creating a more granular ROI for subsequent analyses. As a metric for the evolution of the symptoms between T1 and T2, we calculated the delta-score ( Δ ) in S-FMDRS and CGI-I by subtracting the score at T1 from the score at T2. First, we investigated the relationship between symptom severity and SD BOLD .

chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: lated the delta-score ( Δ ) in S-FMDRS and CGI-I by subtracting the score at T1 from the score at T2. First, we investigated the relationship between symptom severity and SD BOLD . Using the imaging data and clinical scores from the initial assessment and the follow-up we calculated the correlation between Δ SD BOLD of the Δ of symptom severity scores ( Δ CGI-I, Δ S-FMDRS) using Kendall correlation coefficient, as CGI-I and to some extent S-FMDRS, are ordinal data. Second, we built a general linear regression model (GLM) to predict the evolution of the clinical scores at T2 (dependent variable) using the SD BOLD values of the individual ROI ’ s at T1 as independent variable. 3. Results 3.1. Clinical and demographical data We excluded one HC and five patients due to excessive movement during the fMRI (N = 5), one patient due to an anatomical brain lesion (N = 1) and one patient due to current drug abuse (N = 1), and one HC did not complete the imaging acquisition, resulting in a sample size of 79 FND patients and 74 HC. There were no significant differences regarding the demographic data of FND patients and HC (see Table 1 ).

chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: ng acquisition, resulting in a sample size of 79 FND patients and 74 HC. There were no significant differences regarding the demographic data of FND patients and HC (see Table 1 ). FND pa- tients reported significantly higher scores in the BDI and STAI-S (all p- values < 0.001, Table 1 ). As not all patients came back at follow-up, we checked for clinical differences that could represent selection bias. FND patients undergoing follow-up showed no difference in demographic and clinical data compared to FND patients who only participated at inclusion (all p- values < 0.05, Supplementary Table S1 ). Four patients were excluded for the fMRI analyses due to too high motion artefacts resulting in 49 patients included for the fMRI analysis. From those patients who showed A. Schneider et al.

BOLD variability between patients and controls Following the exclusion of subjects with too high motion artefacts, the FND group still differed in terms of number of discarded volumes from HC (FND 5.68 % versus HC 1.61 %, Z = � 5.1, P < 0.001) while no differences were found terms of total FD. Corrected for age and sex, the differences in SD BOLD between FND and HC revealed six significant clusters where FND patients showed higher SD BOLD compared to HC across brain regions including the insula, the hippocampus, the sup- plementary motor area (SMA), the orbitofrontal cortex and the cere- bellum ( Fig. 1 ). The results survived correction for family-wise error on cluster level. Characteristics of these clusters are shown in Supplemen- tary Table S2 . The mapping to the YEO network atlas showed, that the voxels within these clusters were mostly overlapping with the somato- motor network (39 %), the attention/salience networks (22 %), and the limbic network (24 %).

These results remained largely stable when correcting for anxiety and depression ( Supplementary Material , Fig. S2 , Table S4 ). 3.3. Evolution of symptom severity associated with BOLD variability Patients did not differ between M0 and M8 in terms of number of discarded volumes or total FD. To investigate the relationship between BOLD variability and clinical outcomes in FND patients, we extracted the SD BOLD from the main hubs of the three most predominant networks found to be altered in patients compared to HC. For the somatomotor network we selected the SMA, and for the attention networks the insula. The correlation of the Δ symptom severity and Δ SD BOLD showed a significant negative correlation between Δ CGI-1 and the SMA.

chunks.text: d the SMA, and for the attention networks the insula. The correlation of the Δ symptom severity and Δ SD BOLD showed a significant negative correlation between Δ CGI-1 and the SMA. With the CGI-1 ranging from one (no symptoms) to seven (among most extremely ill patients), the Δ CGI-1 can range from � 6 to + 6 with a positive Δ CGI-1 meaning a worse general impression was reported at T2 compared to T1, and a negative Δ CGI-1 meaning an improved general impression was reported at T2 compared to T1. A positive Δ SD BOLD means a higher SD BOLD at T2 compared to T1, and vice versa. Together this indicates that an improvement of the symptom severity represented by a negative Δ CGI-1 correlates with an increased SD BOLD in the SMA at T2 compared to T1 represented by a positive Δ SD BOLD ( Fig. 2 ). There were no sig- nificant correlations with Δ S-FMDRS. In the predictive GLM the SD BOLD in the left Insula could predict Δ CGI-1 ( ß = 0.1, P = 0.041). Thus, a higher SD BOLD at T1 was linked to a positive Δ CGI-1 at T2 indicating a worsening of the symptoms. The were no significant predictions for the S-FMDRS. Results were controlled for age and gender. 4.

Discussion In a cross-sectional as well as longitudinal design, our study aimed to investigate the spatial patterns of BOLD signal variability in FND that may be associated with the severity of symptoms and their potential role as a biomarker for this complex condition. Compared to HC, FND pa- tients presented higher BOLD signal variability in key brain networks, including the somatomotor, salience, and limbic networks, covering regions such as the insula, the hippocampus, and the SMA as well as the orbitofrontal cortex. These findings align with previous neuroimaging research on FND emphasizing aberrant neural activity and disrupted functional connectivity in static measurements ( Drane et al., 2021 ; Perez et al., 2021 ; Pick et al., 2019 ) adding a novel dynamic dimension to our understanding of underlying mechanisms. 4.1.

chunks.file_name: Schneider & Weber - 2024 - BOLD signal variability.pdf
chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: ectivity in static measurements ( Drane et al., 2021 ; Perez et al., 2021 ; Pick et al., 2019 ) adding a novel dynamic dimension to our understanding of underlying mechanisms. 4.1. Critical interplay between somatomotor, salience and limbic networks in FND In FND, altered network connectivity between the somatomotor, salience and limbic networks have frequently been identified and brought in context with the symptomatology of FND suggesting inter- related mechanisms. Functional alterations within these networks were suggested to interfere with a proper preparation and execution of motor functions ( Cojan et al., 2009 ) often associated with emotional arousal ( Aybek et al., 2015 ), combined with an impaired integration of sensory information ( Huys et al., 2021; Pare ´es et al., 2012 ), thus depicting FND as a large-scale brain network dysfunction ( Perez et al., 2012 ). The SMA is a key region of the somatomotor network and is involved in motor planning and execution and has been implicated in FND symptom generation ( Aybek et al., 2015 ; Stone et al., 2007 ).

chunks.file_name: Schneider & Weber - 2024 - BOLD signal variability.pdf
chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: key region of the somatomotor network and is involved in motor planning and execution and has been implicated in FND symptom generation ( Aybek et al., 2015 ; Stone et al., 2007 ). Moreover, the insula as an important hub of the salience network has been impli- cated in various cognitive and affective processes, including inter- oception ( Haruki and Ogawa, 2021 ), emotion regulation ( Gasquoine, 2014 ), and self-awareness ( Modinos et al., 2009; Tisserand et al., 2023 ), for which alterations within these cognitive processes have been pre- viously reported in FND ( Sojka et al., 2021, 2018 ). Moreover, our pre- vious results derived from the same cohort showed altered insular co- activation patterns with the somatomotor as well as default mode network in FND, which was associated with duration of illness as well as stress biomarkers ( Weber et al., 2024 ). Abnormal emotion regulation in FND was previously directly linked to motor outputs: As such, decreased activity was found in the insula and motor regions in patients with functional dystonia in an emotional face fMRI task ( Espay et al., 2018b ).

chunks.file_name: Schneider & Weber - 2024 - BOLD signal variability.pdf
chunks.title: Schneider & Weber 2024 BOLD signal variability
chunks.text: to motor outputs: As such, decreased activity was found in the insula and motor regions in patients with functional dystonia in an emotional face fMRI task ( Espay et al., 2018b ). Further task-based fMRI studies showed that when asked to maintain a grip force while pleasant and unpleasant images were shown, patients showed an amplified force compared to HC towards unpleasant images, together with increased activity in the hippocampus and the posterior cingulate cortex (PCC) ( Blakemore et al., 2016 ).
