The relationships among these three variables are visualized in Supplementary Fig. 1 . Raters correctly identified AI with 81 % sensitivity and 75 % speci- ficity (AUC 0.92). Table 2 The custom prompt developed for ChatGPT-4 to generate the DS. Your Role : You are a resident physician tasked with writing a discharge summary of a patient from a psychiatric clinic from the patient ’ s electronic health records. The diagnoses are: Diagnosis 1, Diagnosis 2, Diagnosis 3 Write a discharge summary from the following notes as a continuous text in complete sentences. Write only the last section “ Procedures ” in bullet points. All sections other than “ Procedures ” should not contain bullet points. Form : Start with the treatment period. Structure the medical summary into 3 sections:

chunks.file_name: discharge-summaries-chatgpt4-2024.pdf
chunks.title: discharge summaries chatgpt4 2024
chunks.text: ” in bullet points. All sections other than “ Procedures ” should not contain bullet points. Form : Start with the treatment period. Structure the medical summary into 3 sections: Diagnoses with corresponding ICD-10 codes, assessment, Procedures. Write the text for each diagnosis in a block of text without structural subdivisions. Diagnoses : Write a short explanatory text under each diagnosis, addressing the following points: Description of the syndrome with relevant psychopathological findings, instrumental findings, and laboratory results, then detailed current medication therapy with justification for indication, precise dosage, observed effects, and side effects, if reported. Mention the start of each medication, changes of dosage, or changes of medication with the date and reason for decision, if apparent from the notes. Write medications as active ingredients and not as brand names. If isolation measures were implemented, mention this. Then explain when and how it was justified. Then write whether the diagnostic criteria according to ICD- 10 are fully met. If they are not fully met, write how many of the necessary criteria are fulfilled. Summary :

chunks.file_name: discharge-summaries-chatgpt4-2024.pdf
chunks.title: discharge summaries chatgpt4 2024
chunks.text: stified. Then write whether the diagnostic criteria according to ICD- 10 are fully met. If they are not fully met, write how many of the necessary criteria are fulfilled. Summary : Then under “ Summary ” , write a summarization of the entire treatment. Explicitly state whether, at the time of discharge, there were any acute indications for self-harm or harm to others, particularly in terms of suicidality. Only if the discharge occurred through absconding, state this fact explicitly. Procedures : Then under “ Procedures, ” write recommendations for further treatment, names of further caregivers, and timing of further treatment, if mentioned in the progress notes. Style : Write the text in the style of a discharge letter using medical terminology. Proceed chronologically, write factually, objectively and concretely, formally, professionally, politely, respectfully. Avoid generalizations, assumptions, verbose and cumbersome formulations, and redundancy. Do not invent or presume anything. Generally, formulate in the imperfect tense, reported speech from individuals (e.g., from patients) in subjunctive I, and medical assessments and diagnoses in the present tense.

chunks.text: 3.2. Main group analysis The main group analysis showed significant overall rating differ- ences between Human and AI in favor of Human ( p < 0.05), rejecting the null hypothesis. Mean ratings were 3.78 [95 % CI 3.75, 3.83] for Human and 3.12 [95 % CI 3.07, 3.15] for AI . The rating distributions of the two main groups are shown in Figs. 2 . Overall, AI ratings tended toward a normal distribution, while Human ratings were skewed more towards the right with a strong mode of 4 (see Fig. 2b ). 3.3. Question-level analysis The above-mentioned rating trends in the main groups were also observed, to a lesser degree at the levels of individual question, and case (see Fig. 3 ). The per-question analysis showed significant rating differences ( p < 0.0033) in favor of Human in all but three questions. These three ex- ceptions were “ relevant recommendations for further non-psychiatric treatment ” (Q8, p = 0.029), “ low proportion of unnecessary informa- tion ” (Q13, p = 0.49) and “ mentioning of relevant safety and legal as- pects ” (Q10, p = 0.69). All other questions favored Human (see Table 4 ).

chunks.file_name: discharge-summaries-chatgpt4-2024.pdf
chunks.title: discharge summaries chatgpt4 2024
chunks.text: oportion of unnecessary informa- tion ” (Q13, p = 0.49) and “ mentioning of relevant safety and legal as- pects ” (Q10, p = 0.69). All other questions favored Human (see Table 4 ). The largest mean rating difference was observed in “ specificity of formulations ” (Q12, Δ 1.19). The second and fourth largest differences occurred in the two prespecified questions most indicative of overall DS quality, “ DS ready to send ” (Q15, Δ 1.12), and “ low expected correction effort ” (Q14, Δ 1.05). For “ DS ready to send ” , when combining ratings ≥ 4 as “ favorable ” and ratings ≤ 2 as “ unfavorable ” , overall approval ratings for Human DS were favorable at 61 %, neutral at 21 %, and unfavorable at 18 %. In contrast, for AI , the proportions were almost reversed (17 % favorable, 27 % neutral, 56 % unfavorable). For “ low expected correction effort ” , approval ratings were slightly higher in both Human and AI ( Human 67 % favorable, 19 % neutral, 14 % unfavorable, vs. AI 22 % favorable, 33 % neutral, 45 % unfavorable). The rating distributions of these two questions are shown in Fig. 4 .

chunks.text: Human 67 % favorable, 19 % neutral, 14 % unfavorable, vs. AI 22 % favorable, 33 % neutral, 45 % unfavorable). The rating distributions of these two questions are shown in Fig. 4 . The other three highest mean rating differences, around Δ 1, were observed in “ quality of the summarization segment ” (Q6, Δ 1.07), “ text coherence ” (Q11, Δ 0.96) and “ mentioning of relevant instrumental and laboratory findings ” (Q11, Δ 0.94). 3.4. Case-level analysis The per-case analysis showed significant rating differences in favor of Human in 16/20 cases ( p < 0.0025). Three cases had no significant rating differences, and one case (C16) had significantly higher ratings for AI (see Table 5 ). 3.5. Rater-level analysis In the per-rater analysis, all raters, except for one (with p = 0.062), rated Human DS significantly higher than AI with p < 0.00625. The rating distributions per rater are depicted in the heatmaps in Fig. 5 . Raters ’ mean ratings are shown in the Supplementary Table 4 . Supplementary Figure 2 provides an overview of all secondary outcomes with graphs correlating the mean ratings of Human with AI per question, case, and rater. 3.6.

chunks.title: discharge summaries chatgpt4 2024
chunks.text: entary Table 4 . Supplementary Figure 2 provides an overview of all secondary outcomes with graphs correlating the mean ratings of Human with AI per question, case, and rater. 3.6. Qualitative review In the unblinded qualitative case-based review, we identified hallu- cinations in 8/20 (40 %) of AI -DS and none in Human -DS. Of these hallucinations, 5 (62.5 %) were determined low clinical relevance, while 3 (37.5 %) were determined high clinical relevance. Additionally, we identified minor content errors in 6 (30 %) AI- DS and 2 (10 %) Human- DS. 4. Discussion For the primary outcome, overall ratings favored Human signifi- cantly (mean Human 3.78 vs. mean AI 3.12), with the key threshold of 3 on our Likert scale marking the transition from neutral to mostly positive ratings. For the secondary outcomes, ratings also favored Human significantly in 12/15 questions. The greatest rating differences were observed in “ specificity of formulations ” ( Δ 1.19), “ quality of the sum- marization segment ” ( Δ 1.07), “ text coherence ” ( Δ 0.96), and the two prespecified questions most indicative of overall DS quality:

chunks.file_name: discharge-summaries-chatgpt4-2024.pdf
chunks.title: discharge summaries chatgpt4 2024
chunks.text: mulations ” ( Δ 1.19), “ quality of the sum- marization segment ” ( Δ 1.07), “ text coherence ” ( Δ 0.96), and the two prespecified questions most indicative of overall DS quality: “ DS ready to send ” ( Δ 1.12) and “ low expected correction effort ” ( Δ 1.05). There were no significant relationships between the two most common di- agnoses, F20.0 and F31.2, their EHR lengths, or their ratings, nor was there a significant dependency between the EHR lengths of all individual cases and their ratings. However, this should be interpreted with caution due to the small sample size and our narrow input token range. Overall, AI succeeded in formulating concisely, as evidenced by the item “ the text contains little unnecessary information ” ( Δ 0.07). It also performed reasonably well in sub-categories related to “ pure Fig. 2a. Violin plot of overall DS ratings with means and medians. Fig. 2b. Bar chart of Human vs. AI DS rating distributions. A. chunks.text: information aggregation ” and formalities, as seen in the items “ assess- ment of clinical features ” ( Δ 0.44), “ assigning information to the correct diagnosis ” ( Δ 0.45), “ appropriate use of medical terminology ” ( Δ 0.48), and “ mentioning of relevant safety and legal aspects ” ( Δ 0.01). The quality ratings dropped noticeably when this information then needed to be contextualized correctly, especially as EHR formulations increased in complexity. This aligns with recent findings indicating that the good performance of LLMs in classic reasoning tasks seems to be largely reliant on reproducing information from pretraining rather than on logical deduction [32,33] . Another clear shortcoming were the clinically relevant hallucinations that occurred in an average of 40 % of AI -DS (none in Human ), of which 37.5 % were judged as “ highly relevant ” . Similar to the study by Williams et al. [18] , most hallucinations occurred in the final “ recommendations section ” . Adhering to the prompt likely became more difficult toward the end of the LLM ’ s response as it neared its context size maximum, a known issue in long-form generation tasks [33,34] .

The unblinded EHR review revealed that in the one case in which AI performed better ( Δ -0.44), the treating resident had changed shortly before the DS was written, giving the new physician little time to familiarize themselves with the patient, which might explain the disparity in performance. chunks.text: with non-significant rating differences in favor of Human ( Δ 0.28) showed very detailed and explicit EHR notes, which presumably facili- tated DS generation for ChatGPT-4. Our study had several limitations, including the small sample size, the token restriction, the low to moderate inter-rater agreement, the use of a non-specialized LLM, and no formal evaluation of information- completeness due to the blinding of raters. However, regarding the last point, residents base their DS not solely on EHR notes, which already impedes direct comparison. Another limitation were the comparatively low approval ratings for “ DS ready to send ” in Human (61 % favorable, 21 % unfavorable). For AI , proportions were almost reversed though (17 % favorable, 56 % unfavorable). The low Human approval ratings may be partially explained by the diverse psychiatric subspecialties of the raters, ranging from acute inpatient closed ward to outpatient treatment, and thus different DS expectations. On the other hand, having blinded raters without in-depth case knowledge, beyond the discharge letters ’ “ clinical assessment ” section, presumably improved rater objectivity.

chunks.file_name: discharge-summaries-chatgpt4-2024.pdf
chunks.title: discharge summaries chatgpt4 2024
chunks.text: ns. On the other hand, having blinded raters without in-depth case knowledge, beyond the discharge letters ’ “ clinical assessment ” section, presumably improved rater objectivity. Other strengths of our study included the use of real-life pseudony- mized EHR data, the custom prompt, and the detailed Likert scale, which allowed for granular insight into the specific strengths and weaknesses of both groups, including ratings for potential time savings during correction. Furthermore, physicians did not know beforehand that their EHR notes would be used as a basis for DS, so our findings likely un- derestimate the potential of LLM-generated DS. For the future, results may improve if EHR notes were written with the specific goal of generating a DS in mind. It also seems expedient to develop custom LLMs specializing in medical DS generation. For instance, an open-source LLM could be fine-tuned on EHR-DS-pairs to specify the use case and to align DS outputs with a clinic ’ s data and standards. This would also address data privacy problems, and pseudonymization might become unnec- essary if computation was kept local.

chunks.file_name: discharge-summaries-chatgpt4-2024.pdf
chunks.title: discharge summaries chatgpt4 2024
chunks.text: align DS outputs with a clinic ’ s data and standards. This would also address data privacy problems, and pseudonymization might become unnec- essary if computation was kept local. Additionally, results could be improved by incorporating different data than EHRs, such as instru- mental diagnostics (high rating difference of Δ 0.94 in this study) and the patient chart. In our internal preliminary tests, ChatGPT-4 was already able to correctly extract information about medication changes and vital parameters from partial patient chart screenshots and some newer LLMs like ChatGPT-4o or the announced “ Project Astra ” by Google process text, photo, and speech information multimodally within one model [35,36] . 5. Conclusion Overall, AI -DS did not achieve quality levels equivalent to those of psychiatric residents but performed comparably in 20 % of individual cases. Our findings of 22 % favorable and 33 % neutral ratings for “ low correction effort ” for AI -DS demonstrate potential for AI -DS to serve as DS-templates, which physicians could finalize, saving time in the future.

chunks.text: 33 % neutral ratings for “ low correction effort ” for AI -DS demonstrate potential for AI -DS to serve as DS-templates, which physicians could finalize, saving time in the future. Our results underscore the need for critical evaluation of LLM-outputs, ideally with in-depth case knowledge, as hallucinations and context errors are still frequent. chunks.text: fact that it is becoming increasingly difficult to distinguish LLM from human outputs. In our study, blinded raters only correctly identified AI - DS with a sensitivity of 81 % and a specificity of 75 %. Future studies should optimize the information basis, specialize the LLM for the use case, ensure assessment of information-completeness and hallucina- tions, and measure time savings to better harness the potential of LLMs in the clinical setting. What was already known on the topic? • DS contribute considerably to physicians ’ documentation burden • Standardization can improve DS quality • AI-DS frequently exhibit hallucinations and may omit clinically relevant information What this study added to our knowledge • Detailed and explicit formulations in EHRs can improve performance of AI-DS (20 % of cases with comparable ratings as residents) • Strengths of AI-DS were information aggregation, conciseness, for- malities, and structure • Weaknesses of AI-DS were specificity, cohesion, and distillation of key information (summarization segment) • With 22 % of AI-DS rated as “ low expected correction effort, ” they show promise as DS-templates in a psychiatric setting Declarations Fundi

chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: 2 Abstract Background: Structured clinical interviews such as the Montgomery-Åsberg Depression Rating Scale (MADRS) are central to depression assessment but are time-intensive and inconsistently applied in routine care. Large language models (LLMs) offer new opportunities to support standardized symptom assessment through conversational interfaces, yet their clinical feasibility, validity, and potential biases remain insufficiently studied. Methods: We evaluated MADRS-Chat, an LLM-assisted text-based chatbot designed to administer the MADRS interview and generate symptom-level scores using a clinician-trained scoring model. In a prospective observational study involving psychiatric in- and outpatients, we evaluated feasibility and acceptability of a conversational chatbot and its agreement with clinician-rated MADRS scores and patient self-rated (MADRS-S) scores. Findings: MADRS-Chat was well accepted by patients and clinicians and chatbot-derived MADRS scores showed meaningful agreement with human ratings. Qualitative feedback indicated that the anonymous nature of the written interaction facilitated more open and detailed symptom disclosure.

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: meaningful agreement with human ratings. Qualitative feedback indicated that the anonymous nature of the written interaction facilitated more open and detailed symptom disclosure. However, chatbot-administered interviews systematically yielded higher severity scores, likely reflecting interaction-induced shifts in response style. This bias was effectively addressed through lightweight post-hoc calibration. Interpretation: Our findings demonstrate that conversational AI can support structured psychiatric assessment in real-world clinical settings when used within clearly defined clinical boundaries. Moreover, text-based conversational interactions can alter symptom reporting, making explicit validation and calibration essential before deployment. Funding: This work was supported by the Swiss National Science Foundation (SNF Grant 205913). This preprint research paper has not been peer reviewed. Electronic copy available at: https://ssrn.com/abstract=6306520 Preprint not peer reviewed

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: 3 Introduction Depression is a prevalent mental health disorder that is frequently underdiagnosed due to non- specific presentations and underreporting, and insufficiently monitored because of limited consultation time, diagnostic complexity, and lack of routine measurement-based follow-up. 1 Structured clinical questionnaires, such as the Montgomery-Åsberg Depression Rating Scale (MADRS2), are well-established tools to assess depressive symptom severity. 3 However, they are time-intensive, require trained personnel, and are often not routinely conducted across psychiatric settings, leading to potential variability in symptom documentation and treatment monitoring.4,5 Recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI), particularly large language models (LLMs), offer new possibilities for improving access to standardized assessments through conversational agents (“chatbots”). These AI-assisted systems may support the collection of symptom-related information in a scalable and resource-efficient manner.

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: ssments through conversational agents (“chatbots”). These AI-assisted systems may support the collection of symptom-related information in a scalable and resource-efficient manner. 6,7 While such tools have shown promise in simulating human-like interaction,8 their application in clinical settings raises important methodological questions regarding validity and acceptability, 9 underscoring the need for clinically grounded implementations. In a recent study, we demonstrated that a fine-tuned BERT (MADRS-BERT) was able to predict individual MADRS subscores with high fidelity, achieving accuracies of up to 88% and a mean absolute error (MAE) as low as 0·7 across items when compared to expert clinical ratings. 10 This symptom-level modeling offered an interpretable and scalable framework to assess depressive severity from structured dialogue. However, despite this progress, MADRS-BERT operated post-hoc on existing transcripts and did not participate in real-time assessment.

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: depressive severity from structured dialogue. However, despite this progress, MADRS-BERT operated post-hoc on existing transcripts and did not participate in real-time assessment. Building on this foundation, we now evaluate whether a text-based LLM conversational agent (CA) can administer the MADRS interview directly to psychiatric in- and outpatients.7 Drawing on established technology acceptance frameworks,11,12 which emphasize perceived usefulness, ease of use, and trust as key determinants of adoption, we examined the feasibility, acceptability, and clinical agreement of this system.13 Unlike MADRS-BERT’s offline scoring, this system engages directly with users, enabling interactive, real-time collection of depressive symptomatology, and investigation of how conversational formats may influence symptom expression. This preprint research paper has not been peer reviewed. Electronic copy available at: https://ssrn.com/abstract=6306520 Preprint not peer reviewed

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: 4 Methods This study evaluated the feasibility, acceptability, and clinical validity of MADRS-Chat, a structured LLM-assisted conversational agent (CA) designed to guide patients through the nine MADRS items while generating item-level scores using the previously developed MADRS- BERT10 model. As MADRS-Chat directly builds on MADRS-BERT, we first externally validated the scoring model by assessing agreement between model-generated and clinician-assigend scores. We then examined the feasibility and acceptability of MADRS-Chat and its agreement with clinician ratings based on the chatbot interview transcripts (Figure 1A). The study was conducted as a single-arm, exploratory, prospective observational study and was conducted in a real-world psychiatric clinical setting. Ethical considerations and participants The study was conducted at the Psychiatric University Hospital Zurich, Switzerland, recruiting psychiatric inpatients and outpatients. Ethical approval was obtained from the local Ethics Committee of Zurich, Switzerland (BASEC: 2025-01252) and conducted according to the Declaration of Helsinki. All participants provided written informed consent.

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: om the local Ethics Committee of Zurich, Switzerland (BASEC: 2025-01252) and conducted according to the Declaration of Helsinki. All participants provided written informed consent. Inclusion criteria were age 18–65 years, fluency in (Swiss-) German, current psychiatric treatment (inpatient, outpatient, or day clinic), ability to provide informed consent, and clinically reported depressive symptoms or a diagnosis of depressive episode (ICD-10: F32·0–F32·2) or recurrent depressive disorder (F33·0–F33·2). Exclusion criteria included organic mental disorders (F01–F09), schizophrenia spectrum and other non-mood psychotic disorders (F20–F29), depressive episodes with psychotic symptoms (F32·3/F33·3), intellectual disability (F70–F79), critical illness, severe head injury, or any condition compromising safety or study compliance. MADRS-BERT external validation A new set of structured MADRS interview data was collected from N=30 psychiatric inpatients and pre-processed as described previously 10 (see Supplementary Material S1 and Supplementary Table 1). MADRS-BERT10 was used to predict item-level scores (0-6).

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: hiatric inpatients and pre-processed as described previously 10 (see Supplementary Material S1 and Supplementary Table 1). MADRS-BERT10 was used to predict item-level scores (0-6). Independently, eight clinicians (AC, NP, LK, BP, JGM, LA, TW, GK), blinded to the model predictions and to each other’s ratings, assigned MADRS item scores based solely on the interview transcript. The reference score per item was defined as the mean clinician rating (Supplementary Table 2). Interrater reliability was calculated using intraclass correlation coefficients (ICC) and Krippendorff’s alpha, evaluating both clinician-clinician agreement and model-clinician agreement. This preprint research paper has not been peer reviewed. Electronic copy available at: https://ssrn.com/abstract=6306520 Preprint not peer reviewed

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: 5 MADRS-Chat Technical Details MADRS-Chat is a structured LLM-assisted CA designed to guide psychiatric patients through a standardized MADRS-based assessment of depressive symptoms. The system follows a predefined rule-based dialogue structure to ensure transparency, control, and patient safety and does not provide diagnostic or therapeutic recommendations. Conversational responses are generated using Llama-3.1-8B (https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct), while separate task-specific models handle classification functions such as sentiment analysis and MADRS scoring (MADRS-BERT10). Interviews were conducted on a laptop under human supervision in clinical settings, with trained personnel able to intervene at any time (Figure 1B; Supplementary Video). Further technical details are provided in Supplementary Material S2. Acceptability and Feasibility At the study visit, patients first completed a self-assessment using MADRS-S3 and subsequently interacted with MADRS-Chat. Acceptability was assessed using structured self-report questionnaires completed by both patients and clinicians.

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: ssment using MADRS-S3 and subsequently interacted with MADRS-Chat. Acceptability was assessed using structured self-report questionnaires completed by both patients and clinicians. Patients rated their experience using a six-items usability and satisfaction questionnaire adapted from established digital health frameworks and prior CA evaluations.6,11,12 In addition, patients completed an adapted version of the six-item Working Alliance Inventory (WAI-6 14), focusing on perceived usefulness, respectfulness, and engagement. Items related to therapeutic progress were omitted, as MADRS- Chat does not provide any interventions. Optional qualitative feedback was collected from patients in free-text format to complement the quantitative findings, focusing on positive aspects of the interaction and potential areas for improvement. Patients received CHF50 (~65US-Dollar) compensation. Clinicians completed a 6-item satisfaction questionnaire adapted from Heinz 6, focused on usability and perceived value of the CA in a psychiatric setting. Furthermore, clinicians evaluated transcript quality (appropriateness, factuality, empathy) using a seven-item Likert scale.

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: and perceived value of the CA in a psychiatric setting. Furthermore, clinicians evaluated transcript quality (appropriateness, factuality, empathy) using a seven-item Likert scale. Lastly, clinicians assigned MADRS item scores from the CA interview transcript using MADRS rating criteria based solely on the verbal content. Feasibility was assessed through completion rates, interview duration, and total word count. All questionnaires are provided in Supplementary Tables 3-5. Analyses were descriptive, as the study was not powered for formal hypothesis testing. Clinician Evaluation To assess clinical agreement, MADRS-Chat derived scores were compared with clinician-rated MADRS and patient self-rated MADRS-S scores at both item and total levels using Pearson correlations, paired statistical tests, and Bland-Altman analyses. This preprint research paper has not been peer reviewed. Electronic copy available at: https://ssrn.com/abstract=6306520 Preprint not peer reviewed

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: 6 Post-hoc calibration on conversational agent interview domain Although MADRS-Chat demonstrated strong agreement with clinician ratings, systematic differences in absolute score levels were observed (see Results), with the CA tending to assign higher severity scores. This pattern was not observed during prior external validation on clinician-led interview transcripts, indicating a context-dependent shift associated with interview modality. The written conversational format elicited more detailed and expressive responses, likely altering the input distribution presented to the scoring model and reflecting a modality-specific covariate shift between clinician-led (oral) and CA-led (written) interviews. To correct for this systematic shift while preserving the underlying scoring model, a post-hoc calibration procedure was applied using clinician ratings as the reference standard. Calibration consisted of a global bias-correction adjustment to account for score inflation while minimally affecting relative differences across participants and items. This approach preserved rank-order information and reduced the risk of overfitting to the clinician-rated calibration sample.

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: ng relative differences across participants and items. This approach preserved rank-order information and reduced the risk of overfitting to the clinician-rated calibration sample. Role of the funding source The funder of the study had no role in study design, data collection, data analysis, data interpretation, or writing of the report. Results Study population Thirty-two (32) patients were recruited. Two did not complete participation (one early discharge, one exclusion due to medical burden), resulting in a final sample of 30 patients. Of these, 26 were inpatients and four outpatients. The sample included N=21 females and mean age was 31±12 years (range 18–61). Detailed demographics are provided in Supplementary Table 6. MADRS-BERT external validation Eight clinicians independently rated the N=30 MADRS interview transcripts. Mean total clinician-rated MADRS scores (mean=16·3, SD=8·6) and MADRS-BERT predictions (mean=18·9, SD=5·5) were comparable across items, with the model assigning slightly higher scores on the topic sleep disturbances, loss of appetite, lassitude, and suicidal ideations. Inter- clinician agreement across the nine MADRS items was high (mean ICC=0·92).

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: higher scores on the topic sleep disturbances, loss of appetite, lassitude, and suicidal ideations. Inter- clinician agreement across the nine MADRS items was high (mean ICC=0·92). Agreement between MADRS-BERT and mean clinician ratings was slightly lower (mean ICC=0·78), indicating that the model aligns closely but not identical with the clinical reference. Krippendorff’s alpha mirrored this pattern, confirming good overall reliability between clinicians and the model. chunks.text: 8 Feasibility Two participants did not complete all topics (7/9 and 5/9) due to technical issues. Mean interview duration was 48·2±19·8 minutes (excluding the first three participants affected by a technical timing artifact). Topic-level analyses showed the longest discussions for suicidal ideation (mean=8·4±4·8min) and pessimistic thoughts (6·3±2·8min), and the shortest for loss of appetite (4·3±2·7 min) and emotional numbness (4·2±2·0min). Patients engaged in 45·1±4·7 conversational turns and produced a mean of 594·9±364·0 words per interview. Item-level, word counts ranged from 48·0±31·4 words (emotional numbness) to 103·2±100·0 words (suicidal ideation). Higher word counts were observed for emotionally salient topics such as suicidal ideation and pessimistic thoughts (84·4±64·6 words), whereas more concrete symptom domains, such as loss of appetite (53·8±44·4 words) and difficulties concentrating (55·6±37·0 words), elicited shorter responses (Table 1). Table 1. Feasibility Measures. Feasibility outcomes include overall interview duration, and mean time spent per MADRS topic. User engagement is summarized by total word count per interview and mean word count per topic.

chunks.text: 9 Acceptability User Acceptance and Session Alliance Patients reported overall positive acceptance and a strong perceived alliance with MADRS- Chat (Figure 2A). Ease of use was rated highly (mean=5·90, SD=1·2; 83% agreement). Ratings of usability (mean=4·3, SD=1·58; 47% agreement) and enjoyment (mean=4·23, SD=1·79; 43% agreement) were more mixed. Alliance-related dimensions were strongly endorsed, particularly respect (mean=6·67, SD=0·84; 97% agreement) and appreciation (mean=5·33, SD=1·69; 80% agreement). Overall satisfaction was moderate (mean=4·53, SD=1·59; 50% agreement). User Satisfaction Patient satisfaction showed a differentiated pattern across interaction features (Figure 2B). Basic usability was rated highly, including ease of learning (mean=6·4, SD=0·77; 97% agreement) and intuitiveness (mean=5·83, SD=1·21; 83% agreement). In contrast, interaction- related aspects were rated lower. Perceived helpfulness was mixed (mean=4·1, SD=1·56; 37% agreement), and conversational naturalness received the lowest ratings, particularly regarding repetition (mean=3·33, SD=1·84; 63% disagreement) and resemblance to a therapist interaction (mean=3·03, SD=1·88; 67% disagreement).

chunks.text: ived the lowest ratings, particularly regarding repetition (mean=3·33, SD=1·84; 63% disagreement) and resemblance to a therapist interaction (mean=3·03, SD=1·88; 67% disagreement). Willingness for personal use was moderate (mean=4·4, SD=1·83; 50% agreement). Clinicians reported consistently positive evaluations (Figure 2C). Ease of learning (mean=6·33, SD=0·66) and intuitiveness (mean=5·8, SD=0·96) received unanimous agreement. Clinical usefulness was rated highly (mean=5·77, SD=1·38; 80% agreement). Most clinicians agreed that the system retrieved relevant information (mean=4·83, SD=1·09; 63% agreement) and would consider using it in practice (mean=5·73, SD=1·62; 77% agreement). Overall satisfaction was high (mean=5·23, SD=1·19; 70% agreement). Clinical Validity Clinician evaluations indicated high perceived response quality across appropriateness (mean=5·87, SD=0·81), factual accuracy (mean = 5·74, SD = 0·79), and empathy (mean = 5·73, SD = 1·05) (Figure 3A). Most ratings were within the agree range (90–94%), with minimal disagreement. Item-level distributions are shown in Supplementary Figure S3.

chunks.text: 10 Bland-Altman analyses showed a systematic positive bias of MADRS-Chat relative to clinician ratings (mean bias=+12·3; limits 1·16 to 23·44) and self-ratings (mean bias=+10·17; limits −6·10 to 26·44). In contrast, clinician and self-ratings demonstrated minimal bias (−2·13; limits −19·46 to 15·19). Following post-hoc bias correction (Methods), mean MADRS-Chat scores (mean=26·6, SD = 6·1) no longer differed significantly from clinician or self-ratings. Bland- Altman analysis confirmed substantial reduction of systematic bias relative to clinician ratings (mean bias=+3·53; limits −7·30 to 14·36) and self-ratings (mean bias=+1·40; limits −14·12 to 16·92). Corresponding Bland-Altman plots for total scores are provided in the Supplementary Figures S4 and S5. Table 2 presents item-level and total MADRS comparisons between clinician ratings and MADRS-Chat, as well as bias-corrected MADRS-Chat, including item- level P-values adjusted for multiple comparisons using false discovery rate (FDR). Analyses involving patient self-ratings (self-rated MADRS-S vs. MADRS-Chat, vs. bias-corrected MADRS-Chat, and vs. clinician ratings) are provided in the Supplementary Tables 7 and 8.

chunks.text: 12 Semi-structured qualitative feedback In open-ended feedback, anonymity emerged as a central theme. Many participants reported that the written format allowed them to write more openly about sensitive experiences, facilitating honesty and disclosure without fear of being judged. Writing rather than speaking was described as helpful for structuring thoughts, reflecting on emotions, and articulating experiences that would be difficult to express in person. Patients further emphasized the CA’s empathetic and responsive interaction style, describing a sense of being heard and understood. The polite, non-intrusive tone, reflective summaries, and targeted follow-up questions were frequently contrasted with static questionnaires and experienced as more engaging and supportive for self-reflection. Additional positive aspects included clear and accessible language, robustness to typos, conversational continuity, and the inclusion of crisis hotline information, which was perceived as reassuring. In contrast, patients identified several areas for improvement.

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: ypos, conversational continuity, and the inclusion of crisis hotline information, which was perceived as reassuring. In contrast, patients identified several areas for improvement. The most frequently mentioned issue was excessive repetition, including repeated phrasing, mirroring, and follow-up questions, which some participants found tiring or disruptive to the conversational flow. Some participants reported difficulties with contextual understanding, especially when responses contained multiple elements, and occasional response latency. Suggestions for improvement focused on greater conciseness, reduced repetition, and more adaptive conversational flow. Discussion In this study, we evaluated the feasibility, acceptability, and clinical agreement of MADRS- Chat, an LLM-based CA administering the MADRS in a real-world psychiatric setting. Two main findings emerged. First, MADRS-Chat was feasible and well accepted. Patients reported high ease of use and perceived alliance, particularly feeling treated respectfully, and clinicians rated responses as appropriate, accurate, and empathetic.

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: pted. Patients reported high ease of use and perceived alliance, particularly feeling treated respectfully, and clinicians rated responses as appropriate, accurate, and empathetic. These findings indicate that a structured, symptom-focused conversational interview can be integrated into psychiatric care without compromising interaction quality. Second, MADRS-Chat scores showed meaningful agreement with clinician and self-ratings but demonstrated a systematic positive bias in absolute severity. This bias was not observed in prior validation on clinician-led transcripts, suggesting a modality-dependent effect. Qualitative feedback indicated that the written, anonymous format elicited more detailed and emotionally expressive disclosures. Post-hoc calibration corrected this shift while preserving relative differences, supporting the interpretation that the discrepancy reflected calibration rather than loss of validity. Together, these results position MADRS-Chat This preprint research paper has not been peer reviewed. Electronic copy available at: https://ssrn.com/abstract=6306520 Preprint not peer reviewed

chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: 13 as a complementary tool for structured symptom assessment, particularly for standardized monitoring or pre-visit screening. Most prior conversational AI systems in mental health have focused on intervention-oriented applications,6,15,16 such as emotional support, psychoeducation, or behavioral change, rather than structured clinical assessment. 17,18 Across these studies, users generally report high ease of use, perceived support, and appreciation for the low-threshold nature of chatbot interactions, while recurrent barriers include repetition, interaction fatigue, response latency, and declining engagement over time. Our findings align closely with this literature. MADRS-Chat demonstrated high acceptability and perceived alliance among patients, as well as strong clinician endorsement of response quality. At the same time, open-ended feedback highlighted similar limitations, particularly repetitiveness and occasional contextual misunderstandings. Importantly, these constraints may have different implications depending on the system’s purpose.

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: itations, particularly repetitiveness and occasional contextual misunderstandings. Importantly, these constraints may have different implications depending on the system’s purpose. In intervention-focused systems, sustained engagement and perceived therapeutic benefit are central, making repetitiveness particularly detrimental. 19 In contrast, for structured assessment and monitoring, interactions are time-limited and goal-directed, potentially making such limitations more tolerable. In terms of assessment design, many prior approaches have adapted validated questionnaires such as PHQ-9 and GAD-7 20 into structured conversational formats. While these studies demonstrate that conversational delivery can preserve overall psychometric properties, they typically emphasize total-score agreement and rely on fixed prompt sequences. Notably, amplification of symptom severity has been observed in some CA-based assessments, 18,21 underscoring that conversational formats may introduce modality-dependent effects requiring calibration.

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: n of symptom severity has been observed in some CA-based assessments, 18,21 underscoring that conversational formats may introduce modality-dependent effects requiring calibration. Evidence from intervention-focused CA research suggests that effectiveness depends less on whether systems are rule-based or LLM-based, and more on how structure, constraint, and interactional flexibility are combined.22 Together, these findings support hybrid architectures that preserve clinical structure while allowing conversational flexibility, 23 aligning closely with the design of MADRS-Chat, where conversational guidance and symptom scoring were architecturally separated. The generative model guided disclosure, while severity ratings were assigned by a domain-specific model trained on clinician-rated MADRS interviews. Together, these studies support a growing consensus that AI systems for psychiatric assessment benefit most from hybrid architectures, i.e., using LLM-based conversational interfaces for data collection while leaving scoring and potential interpretation to a clinically grounded, domain-specific model. chunks.text: 14 Although MADRS-Chat demonstrated feasibility and strong agreement with human ratings, the conversational format introduced a systematic positive bias in absolute severity scores. Prior work in medical AI has shown that even when LLMs align closely with guidelines or expert judgments, they may exhibit stable directional distortions driven by contextual framing, input characteristics, or interaction dynamics. 24,25. Consistent with this, Bland-Altman analyses revealed that MADRS-Chat rated symptoms significantly higher than both clinician- and self- ratings. Importantly, this bias was not observed during prior validation on clinician-led interview transcripts, suggesting that it was not intrinsic to the scoring model but emerged from the conversational assessment modality itself. The written, interactive format likely altered the linguistic input distribution, constituting a modality-specific covariate shift. 26 Notably, this interaction-induced bias was effectively corrected through a lightweight post-hoc calibration, aligning absolute score levels while preserving rank-order correlations.

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: otably, this interaction-induced bias was effectively corrected through a lightweight post-hoc calibration, aligning absolute score levels while preserving rank-order correlations. This indicates that the discrepancy reflected calibration rather than loss of clinical validity and highlights the necessity of bias-aware validation when conversational systems are used for symptom measurement. 24 More broadly, risks in LLM-based mental health systems appear to increase when interaction, reasoning, and decision-making are tightly coupled.27 In line with this, MADRS-Chat separates the conversational model from the scoring model, 10 thereby constraining where and how bias can arise. Within this constrained scope, bias becomes measurable, interpretable, and correctable, supporting the view that to date conversational AI may be particularly suited for standardized symptom assessment and monitoring. Lastly, it is important to consider why this systematic bias emerged. Beyond model characteristics, our findings suggest that the conversational format itself shaped symptom disclosure.

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: y, it is important to consider why this systematic bias emerged. Beyond model characteristics, our findings suggest that the conversational format itself shaped symptom disclosure. Compared with clinician-led interviews, patients interacting with MADRS-Chat produced longer, more detailed, and emotionally expressive responses. Many reported that writing felt easier than speaking and enabled articulation of thoughts they might otherwise withhold. This pattern aligns with qualitative evidence indicating that conversational AI is often experienced as emotionally safe and disinhibiting, partly due to perceived anonymity. 28 Clinically, such increased openness provides a plausible explanation for the observed inflation in severity scores: richer language may amplify item-level signals without reflecting a true increase in underlying symptom burden. Notably, this interaction dynamic resembles elements of a digital therapeutic alliance,29 including perceived empathy, respect, and feeling heard, even in a system explicitly designed for assessment rather than therapy.

chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026

chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: 15 Several limitations should be considered. First, the study was primarily designed to assess feasibility and acceptability, and the sample size was not powered for definitive conclusions regarding clinical validity. Larger studies are required to robustly evaluate item-level reliability, subgroup effects, and generalizability across settings. Second, detailed sociodemographic variables were not systematically collected. Although the clinical sample was heterogeneous, the absence of structured data on socioeconomic background, ethnicity, and gender or sexual minority status limits conclusions regarding equity and differential performance. In addition, participation may have introduced selection bias, as individuals willing to enroll could be more receptive to AI-based tools; however, financial compensation may have mitigated this effect. Third, MADRS-Chat was developed prior to the widespread adoption of reasoning-optimized LLM architectures and does not explicitly implement multi-step reasoning or chain-of-thought prompting.30 Future studies should examine whether such approaches provide incremental value beyond structured clinical grounding.

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: mplement multi-step reasoning or chain-of-thought prompting.30 Future studies should examine whether such approaches provide incremental value beyond structured clinical grounding. Fourth, the evaluation was cross-sectional and limited to a single session. The stability, sensitivity to change, and potential drift of symptom estimates during longitudinal use remain unknown and should be examined in future monitoring studies. Fifth, both the CA and transcript-based clinician ratings relied exclusively on written content, whereas several MADRS items traditionally incorporate non-verbal cues such as facial expression, prosody, and psychomotor behavior. Consequently, in-person clinician ratings might differ from transcript-based assessments. Future work should explore multimodal extensions. Finally, calibrating MADRS-Chat to clinician ratings enhances comparability with current clinical standards but does not eliminate potential bias inherent to clinician judgment itself. Interaction-level limitations, including repetitiveness and response latency, were also identified and may become more relevant in repeated use contexts.

chunks.file_name: madrs-chat-ssrn-2026.pdf
chunks.title: madrs chat ssrn 2026
chunks.text: clinician judgment itself. Interaction-level limitations, including repetitiveness and response latency, were also identified and may become more relevant in repeated use contexts. In conclusion, this work positions MADRS-Chat within a growing but still underdeveloped space between traditional clinician-led assessment and fully autonomous mental-health CAs. Our findings support the view that structured assessment represents an accepted, and clinically tractable application of conversational AI in psychiatry. Crucially, this study shows that conversational assessment is not a neutral replacement of existing formats: the interaction itself shapes symptom expression. Anonymity-driven openness and emotional richness can enhance sensitivity, but also systematically shift severity estimates. This highlights that bias-aware validation and calibration are essential when conversational systems are used for clinical measurement. By separating the conversational model from symptom scoring models, MADRS- Chat demonstrates a pragmatic pathway for integrating LLMs into psychiatric workflows.

chunks.text: 20 Figure legends Fig. 1 | Study overview, conversational agent interface, and external validation of MADRS-BERT. (A) Schematic of the study workflow. Patients completed a structured MADRS interview using MADRS-Chat; feasibility and acceptability were assessed from both patient and clinician perspectives, and clinicians additionally evaluated the professional quality of conversational agent generated interviews based on transcript review. (B) Example screenshots of the MADRS-Chat interface illustrating the guided assessment (e.g., items on sleep and inner tension). A video can be found in Supplementary Material. (C) External validation of MADRS-BERT: mean MADRS item scores across topics shown as mean ± standard deviation (SD), with each line representing one rater (•black = MADRS-BERT; colored = clinicians) and shaded bands indicating ±1 SD. Fig. 2 | User acceptance, session alliance, and satisfaction with MADRS-Chat. Diverging stacked Likert distributions summarizing acceptance and satisfaction ratings on a 7-point Likert scale.

chunks.text: er acceptance, session alliance, and satisfaction with MADRS-Chat. Diverging stacked Likert distributions summarizing acceptance and satisfaction ratings on a 7-point Likert scale. (A) Patient-reported user acceptance and session alliance, capturing perceived ease of use, usability, enjoyment, and alliance with the conversational agent. (B) Patient satisfaction with MADRS-Chat, including perceived helpfulness, interaction quality, and willingness to use the system. (C) Clinician satisfaction ratings evaluating the conversational agent from a professional perspective, including perceived usefulness, information quality, and overall satisfaction. Percentages indicate the proportion of responses per Likert category (1–7), with negative, neutral, and positive response options displayed from left to right (only values above 10% are displayed). Fig. 3 | Clinical validity of MADRS-Chat compared with self- and clinician-rated MADRS. (A) Clinician-rated evaluation of MADRS-Chat responses across all MADRS items, summarised over the dimensions Appropriateness, Factual Accuracy, and Empathy.

Panels show pairwise comparisons between self-rated MADRS and MADRS-Chat, self-rated MADRS and bias-corrected MADRS- Chat, clinician-rated MADRS and MADRS-Chat, and clinician-rated MADRS and bias- corrected MADRS-Chat. Each point represents one subject; solid lines indicate the line of identity. chunks.text: Table 2. Item-level and total-score comparisons between MADRS-Chat and clinician-rated MADRS. Values are reported as mean ± standard deviation (SD) for clinician ratings, MADRS-Chat, and bias-corrected MADRS- Chat. Associations between MADRS-Chat and clinician ratings are quantified using Pearson correlation coefficients (r). Differences between conversational agent ratings and clinician ratigns were assessed using Wilcoxon signed-rank tests, with test statistics (W) and associated P-values. For item-level analyses, P-values were adjusted for multiple comparisons using the Benjamini–Hochberg false discovery rate (FDR) procedure.
